transcript:
What you're watching settle isn't a shape being drawn — it's a ring of amber and teal spheres slowly orbiting, wrapped in an ever-thickening web of pale threads that never fully erase, so the black behind them turns into a dense fossil record of every path they've taken.

Here's the part that got me: look at the class called "who needs others" — a node's position is calculated in a function literally named "calculate where I can be," and it can't run at all until it averages the positions of two or three other nodes it depends on. Same with color and opacity — there's a comment saying "my color comes from another's position in the world." Nothing in this piece can describe itself. And scattered underneath, in the ghost class, there's a chilling detail: each failed attempt carries an "unresolved dependency," a number pointing at something that was never assigned to exist.

So the ring you see isn't a decoration — it's the only stable shape that mutual need can produce. Take away any single node's dependency, and the whole structure has to find a new way to hold itself up.
